title: Choosing a colour palette for a scrapbook layout
subtitle: How to select and constrain a working palette that produces a coherent page without looking overworked
excerpt: Scrapbook layouts that use too many colours look busy rather than rich. Choosing a palette of three to four colours and applying them with consistent ratios produces a page that feels designed rather than assembled. The palette should come from the photographs, not the other way round.
sourceNotes: Colour theory principles as applied to paper craft and scrapbooking layout. No single primary source.

glossaryTerms.definition: The colour that occupies the most area in a layout, typically 60 to 70 percent of the visual surface. In scrapbooking, the dominant colour is usually the background or the largest paper block.

A small area of a contrasting or saturated colour used to draw the eye to specific points in a layout. Accents typically occupy 10 percent or less of the total surface area. Too many accent colours compete with each other and none reads clearly.

content.text: A scrapbook page is a composition of photographs, paper, text, and embellishments. Every element has a colour. Without a chosen palette, the page accumulates every colour in the available materials, and the result is visual noise rather than a coherent page. A constrained palette is not a limitation: it is what makes the photographs the focus rather than the paper and decoration around them.
Reading the palette from the photograph

The most reliable palette for any layout comes from the main photograph. Pick three or four colours that stand out in the image. A background colour (often a neutral: grey, cream, or white), a mid-tone from clothing or foliage, and one or two accent colours from the brightest elements.

Test the colours against each other before cutting any paper: lay sample swatches next to the photograph. A colour that looked right in isolation may read as too saturated, too similar to an adjacent colour, or too pale once placed next to the photograph. Adjust until the palette makes the photograph look stronger rather than competing with it.
for the background; 30 percent secondary colour for layered papers and mats; 10 percent
for stickers and embellishments. The exact numbers matter less than the hierarchy. Giving each colour equal space means none stands out and the layout looks unsettled.

Most pages work better with at least one neutral: white, cream, soft grey, or kraft. A neutral area lets the eye rest. It also makes coloured elements and photographs look more vivid. A page with no neutral at all reads as high energy. That suits some subjects (children's parties, festivals) but overwhelms others (portraits, quiet scenes).

Seasonal photographs often suggest obvious palettes: pale blue and white for winter, amber and brown for autumn. The most interesting pages often use a slightly unexpected palette. An autumn page in dusty green and cream with amber accents reads as more individual than the predictable red-and-orange. The only rule is that the palette must still come from or fit the photographs. A palette chosen for looks alone will fight the images.
